Regan Smith looking on after the women's 200m butterfly final on Day Six of the 2024 United States Olympic Team Swimming Trials at Lucas Oil Stadium on June 20 in Indianapolis, Indiana.

PHOTO: AFP

Published脗Jun 23, 2024, 04:25 PM

Updated脗Jun 23, 2024, 07:50 PM

LOS ANGELES芒聙聯Regan Smith is back and ready for battle at the Paris Olympics, where she thinks a 100m backstroke duel with Australian Kaylee McKeown could produce a historic result.

Smith, 22, clocked 57.13 seconds to win the event at the US Olympic swimming trials last week, regaining the world record she first claimed as a 17-year-old in 2019 芒聙聯 when she also set a 200m back world record.

A crisis of confidence and drop in form saw Smith settle for two silver medals and a bronze at the pandemic-delayed Tokyo Olympics, where fellow 22-year-old McKeown won the 100m and 200m backstroke.

The Aussie, who first claimed the 100m back world record in 2021, lowered both the 100m and 200m marks in 2023 and clocked the second-fastest 200m backstroke time ever at the Australian trials in June.

Smith, who has been re-energised by working with coach Bob Bowman, former mentor of Olympic superstar Michael Phelps, was thrilled to break out of her backstroke doldrums weeks before an expected meeting with the Australian star on the sport芒聙聶s biggest stage.

芒聙聹I think 56 (seconds) is a possibility, for sure,芒聙聺 Smith said. 芒聙聹Whether it芒聙聶s me or one of my competitors, who knows. But I芒聙聶m not going to sell myself short, absolutely not.芒聙聺

Only five women have broken 58 seconds in the 100m back and all of them will be in Paris: Smith, McKeown, Aussie Mollie O芒聙聶Callaghan, Canadian Kylie Masse and American Katharine Berkoff, who joined the club with her runner-up finish to Smith at the trials.

Even if O芒聙聶Callaghan opts out of the backstroke to focus on other events, it promises to be a thriller, and Smith expects to be in the thick of it.

芒聙聹That was an amazing race but it wasn芒聙聶t a perfect race,芒聙聺 she said of her record-setting trials final. 芒聙聹I know there芒聙聶s things that I can clean up and do better, and I芒聙聶m going to work towards that.芒聙聺

Smith, who also won the 200m butterfly and 200m backstroke in Indianapolis to line up a trio of individual events in Paris, said the feeling of her world record swim was 芒聙聹so different芒聙聺 to her first, five years ago at the world championships.

芒聙聹When you芒聙聶re 17, when you芒聙聶re a teenager and I had not really done much to my name yet 芒聙聯 it was very easy,芒聙聺 she said. 芒聙聹I had no pressure on me. Nobody really expected much out of me, and so it was so easy to walk into races feeling so fearless and not really caring what the outcome was.

芒聙聹I really just shocked myself at that meet in 2019 because I didn芒聙聶t believe I was capable of it. Now tonight, I芒聙聶m in a much different place in my life. I芒聙聶m a lot older, obviously. The pressure is a lot different.

芒聙聹But I think I芒聙聶ve learnt a lot over these five years, and I芒聙聶ve had a lot of lows, in backstroke, in particular.芒聙聺

Smith said she felt it was a mental block that kept her from progressing in the backstroke.

芒聙聹For a long time, I viewed swimming as like a job, and it was a business trip. I think there芒聙聶s a time and a place for that. Yes, I have a job to do, but at the same time this is just fun, and thank God it芒聙聶s a sport. This isn芒聙聶t life or death.

芒聙聹I just want to lean into the fun side 100 per cent and I think that just makes it a lot easier to go out there and let rip and give your best effort every single time. And I know Kaylee will.芒聙聺

Meanwhile on June 22, Caeleb Dressel powered to victory in the 100m butterfly at the trials to line up another title defence in Paris, where Katie Ledecky will go for a historic fourth straight 800m freestyle gold.

Said Dressel: 芒聙聹I think I芒聙聶ve felt every emotion that I芒聙聶m capable of. It芒聙聶s tough. So really the motto is one stroke at a time.芒聙聺AFP, REUTERS

title: American swimmer Regan Smith fired up for backstroke battles at Paris Olympics | The Straits Times
description: She thinks a 100m backstroke duel with Australian Kaylee McKeown there could produce a historic result. Read more at straitstimes.com.
